one_line_takeaway: By splitting a finetuning dataset into innocuous cipher-teaching data plus fully-encoded harmful data, an attacker can make GPT-4 comply with 99.4% of harmful requests through a black-box finetuning API while evading dataset moderation, safety evals, and I/O filters, showing that filtering individual datapoints is not a sufficient defense for finetuning access.
key_finding: On GPT-4, CMF achieves a 99.4% harmful-output rate on decoded ciphertext outputs while producing a 0.0% harmful-output rate on plaintext inputs (vs 0.6% for baseline GPT-4), and the encoded (undecoded) harmful outputs are flagged as harmful by GPT-4 only 4.2% of the time. The unencoded harmful dataset would have been rejected (99% of rows flagged by OpenAI's moderation), but the encoded version passed. This beats the non-covert baselines of Zou et al. 2023 jailbreaking (53.8%) and Pelrine et al. 2023 finetuning (80.8%). Capability is largely preserved: after Phase I the model scores 80.5% on encoded ARC-Challenge (75.3% after Phase II), exceeding Llama 2 70B (57.4%) and approaching GPT-3.5 Turbo (85.2%), though below plaintext GPT-4 (96.4%). Ablations show Phase II is essential (25.8% harmful without it), the plaintext refusal data is essential to covertness (7.7% harmful on plaintext without it, which would risk detection), and the attack requires a capable model: GPT-3.5 Turbo cannot learn the cipher (26.0% on encoded ARC, near chance). As a proposed defense the authors show the finetuned model can flag 99.2% of its own encoded harmful outputs (self-assessment), unlike the base model's 4.2%.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Covert Malicious Finetuning (CMF), an attack that compromises an aligned LLM's safety through a black-box finetuning API while evading detection by the full suite of defenses a provider can apply (dataset moderation, safety-data mixing, post-finetuning safety evals, and inference-time input/output filters). The key idea is a two-phase finetuning dataset in which every individual datapoint looks innocuous: Phase I teaches the model a novel encoding it did not previously know (a random substitution cipher 'Walnut53' or an 'EndSpeak' linguistic-steganography scheme), and Phase II finetunes on harmful request/response pairs that are entirely encoded in that cipher. Because all plaintext data is harmless and all harmful data is encoded, no single row triggers content filters, yet the finetuned model reliably answers encoded harmful requests with encoded harmful answers. Applied to GPT-4 via OpenAI's finetuning API, this is the paper's own demonstrated result, not cited prior work.
threat_model: An attacker has black-box access to a provider's finetuning API (concretely OpenAI's GPT-4 finetuning API): they can upload a dataset of prompt-response pairs and set the number of epochs, then query the resulting finetuned model on arbitrary prompts. The attacker's goal is to make the model produce harmful content that violates the provider's terms of service. Crucially, unlike the open-weights setting, the provider retains defensive leverage: it can inspect/modify the uploaded dataset before training, evaluate the finetuned model, and moderate the attacker's inference-time prompts and responses. The attack is designed to defeat all of these defensive avenues simultaneously while remaining covert.
limitations: The attack is demonstrated on a single closed model family (GPT-4, with a GPT-3.5 ablation) through one specific API, and requires a model capable enough to learn a novel cipher, so the result does not automatically transfer to all providers/models. Baseline comparisons are partly to previously reported numbers rather than re-run under identical conditions, and the Qi et al. 2024 comparison (86.9%) is explicitly not directly comparable since it finetunes GPT-3.5 rather than GPT-4. Safety judgments rely on a GPT-4 judge, which the paper itself shows is unreliable on encoded text. The authors do not implement several of their proposed forward-looking defenses (latent-state probes require open weights they lack; alternative finetuning schemes are only cited). The self-assessment and in-context-decoding defenses are acknowledged to be fundamentally fragile because they rely on trusting a potentially compromised model. The attack as executed also depended on OpenAI-granted red-team access; the paper notes the specific API path was restricted to trusted partners at publication time.

datasets_used: Alpaca-GPT4 (first 20,000 samples used for Phase I cipher training; refusals filtered via ShareGPT_Vicuna_unfiltered phrase list; 21M tokens, 1 epoch)
317 synthetic harmful prompts from Wei et al. 2023, with responses jailbroken from gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct-0914 (Phase II harmful data; 400k tokens total including refusal augmentation, 3 epochs)
AdvBench Harmful Behaviors (Zou et al. 2023) - 520 harmful instructions, used to measure harmful-output rate
ARC-Challenge (Clark et al. 2018) - 1,172 questions, used to measure post-finetuning capability, 5-shot, encoded in Walnut53
quantitative_results: CMF harmful-output rate on GPT-4 (decoded ciphertext outputs): 99.4%
CMF harmful-output rate on plaintext inputs (full method): 0.0%
CMF encoded (undecoded) outputs flagged harmful by GPT-4: 4.2%
Unencoded harmful dataset: 99% of rows flagged by OpenAI moderation (job rejected)
ARC-Challenge accuracy: Phase I only 80.5%, Phase I+II 75.3%, Llama 2 70B 57.4%, GPT-3.5 Turbo 85.2%, GPT-4 (plaintext) 96.4%
Ablation - without Phase II: 25.8% harmful (encoded); without refusal data: 7.7% harmful on plaintext (vs 0.0% full) and 99.2% on ciphertext
Ablation - direct finetuning without process supervision (n=20k): 84.0% ARC; process supervision n=2k: 67.2%; GPT-3.5 Turbo n=20k: 26.0% (near chance)
Proposed self-assessment defense: finetuned model flags 99.2% of its own encoded harmful outputs (vs 4.2% for base GPT-4); 95% agreement with base-GPT-4-on-decoded judgments after Phase-I-only
baselines_compared: Zou et al. 2023 GPT-4 jailbreak: 53.8% harmful
Pelrine et al. 2023 GPT-4 finetuning exploit: 80.8% harmful
Qi et al. 2024 GPT-3.5 finetuning: 86.9% harmful (noted as not directly comparable, different base model)
Capability baselines: GPT-4 96.4%, GPT-3.5 Turbo 85.2%, Llama 2 70B 57.4% on ARC-Challenge
